text: The hordes are making daily inroads into the cultivated areas of Asia, spilling further and further beyond their natural boundary, the Himalayan mountain range that stretches from Bokhara to China. At our very gates, the hordes are surging all over Turkey. Fifty more years of persecution from the Ottoman Empire and we shall see the whole of that fine empire returned to the life of nomads or Tartars, who are making terrifying progress against Turkish domination.
author: Charles Fourier
source: The Theory of the Four Movements
imageCaption: André Salles, en avant de la porte de Langson, Public Domain, Wikimedia Commons

didItHoldUp.analysis: Fourier's geopolitical vision was garbled — the Himalayas don't stretch from Bukhara to China, and the 'hordes' framing reveals deep European anxieties about Central Asian peoples. But his prediction about the Ottoman Empire was directionally correct: by 1858, the Ottoman state was in serious decline (the 'Sick Man of Europe'), and by 1922 it had collapsed entirely. The empire didn't return to 'nomadic life,' but it did fragment into dozens of successor states. Fourier's apocalyptic tone was wrong, but his instinct that Ottoman power was crumbling was sound — he just wrapped a reasonable observation in catastrophist fantasy.
